properties.mechanism: Carts that are abandoned never convert into placed orders, so a higher abandonment rate pulls down the daily order count.
Abandoned cart value is lost potential revenue, so rising abandonment depresses daily revenue.
Abandoned carts reduce the volume of orders entering the top of the fulfillment funnel.
The set of available payment methods affects whether shoppers complete checkout, so payment friction drives cart abandonment.
Shipping cost and delivery options are a leading reason shoppers abandon carts at checkout.
The funnel's stage counts (Orders, Invoiced, Shipped, Completed) are computed from counts of orders grouped by order_status, which is exactly what Order Status Distribution provides.
Orders that progress to the Shipped/Completed stages of the funnel form the population eligible to be refunded, so funnel throughput drives refund volume.
Periods with more orders flowing through the fulfillment funnel co-move with higher summed order revenue, since completed orders generate revenue.
Period revenue equals order count times average order value, so the daily order count is a multiplicative component of summed order_total.
Revenue = Orders × AOV, so average order value is the per-order component of total period revenue.
Summing line-item revenue across all products reconstructs the period's total order revenue, making product revenue a partition component.
Category revenue partitions the same line-item revenue and sums to the period total order revenue.
Revenue grouped by RFM segment re-sums the same order totals and adds up to the period's total revenue.
New customer registrations expand the active buyer base, generating additional orders that lift period revenue.
Abandoned carts are orders that fail to convert, so higher abandonment suppresses realized period revenue.
Higher retention means more returning customers placing repeat orders, increasing period revenue.
Marketing actions prioritized toward high-value segments drive additional orders and thus period revenue.
Rising churn risk reduces future repeat purchasing, dragging down subsequent period revenue.
Both track the same store's total monthly revenue, so they reliably co-move despite different platform sources.
The blended daily revenue trend aggregates the same underlying sales, co-moving with monthly Magento order revenue.
Both metrics count Magento orders grouped by their status/stage (Orders, Invoiced, Shipped, Completed), so the per-status counts in the distribution move in lockstep with the funnel stage counts.
The 'refunded' bucket of the order status distribution is the same set of orders counted by the refund analysis's COUNT(orders WHERE status = refunded), so the two co-move.
Both count the same Magento order events grouped by hour_of_day, with the heatmap simply adding a day_of_week dimension, so they co-move tightly.
Daily order counts are the same underlying Magento orders aggregated at a coarser time grain, so hourly and daily order volumes reliably co-move.
This daily order count and the hourly order count are derived from the same Magento order events at different time granularities, so they track each other.
A higher volume of orders placed within a period mechanically generates more total revenue for that period.
Both metrics partition the same underlying set of Magento orders by a categorical attribute, so their per-category counts rise and fall together with overall order volume.
Both are COUNT(orders) grouped by a dimension over the identical order base, so their totals co-move with total order volume.
The payment-method counts sum to the total daily order count, so the breakdown co-moves with overall order volume.
The subject's Refund Count = COUNT(orders WHERE status = refunded) is the 'refunded' slice of the order-status counts, so it is computed directly from the order status distribution.
Refunded order amounts subtract from realized daily revenue, so a higher refund rate/amount pulls down net revenue on the same Magento store.
Both metrics count the same set of Magento orders partitioned by a categorical attribute, so their per-bucket counts rise and fall together with overall order volume.
Order-status counts and shipping-method counts are drawn from the same order population, so both move in lockstep as total orders change.
Total daily order count and the per-shipping-method counts share the same underlying orders, so the method breakdown co-moves with overall order volume.
Category Performance sums line_item_revenue grouped by product_category, so each category's revenue is an aggregation of the per-product line_item_revenue that defines Top Products.
Total daily store revenue and the revenue of top-selling products are computed from the same Magento order line items, so they reliably co-move.
Period order revenue and top-product revenue draw on the same Magento sales, so strong product months coincide with high overall revenue.
Cross-sell recommendations increase co-purchases of associated products, lifting those products' line_item_revenue and their ranking among top products.
Total daily store revenue is the sum of revenue across all product categories, so category performance directly aggregates into the store revenue trend.
Total daily store orders are composed of orders spanning the product categories, so category-level order volume aggregates into the overall orders trend.
New customer registrations add distinct customer_ids to the base, increasing the count of new/registered customers in the mix.
Higher retention keeps customers active and reordering, shifting the customer mix toward returning customers versus one-time buyers.
Both metrics partition the same distinct customer base into groups, so the distribution of customers by type co-moves with the distribution across RFM segments.
Both metrics are aggregations of the same Magento order events counted by time, so the heatmap's hourly cells move in lockstep with the hour-of-day order counts.
Daily order counts are the sum of the heatmap's cells across all hours for each day, so the two reliably co-move as views of the same order volume.
Both count the same underlying Magento orders, with daily totals equaling the heatmap aggregated over hours, so they statistically track each other.
This is the same daily order-count metric computed on the same Magento data, so the two move together identically.
More daily orders mechanically generate more daily revenue since each order contributes its order_total to the revenue sum.
AOV is Total Revenue divided by Total Orders, so the daily order count is the denominator component of the AOV formula.
Daily order count is the sum of the hourly order counts grouped by hour_of_day.
Daily orders aggregate the per-day-of-week-and-hour order counts that make up the heatmap.
The daily order count equals the sum of order counts across all order_status buckets.
Placed orders are the top-of-funnel input that feeds into the invoiced, shipped, and completed fulfillment stages.
Today's blended order count tracks the same purchase events, so the Magento daily order trend co-moves with it.
A larger volume of daily orders increases the pool of orders that can subsequently be refunded.
Daily revenue equals daily order count multiplied by average order value, so the order count is a direct multiplicative component of revenue.
Daily revenue equals average order value multiplied by the number of orders, so AOV is the per-order value component of the revenue identity.
Both metrics sum order_total over time, so daily revenue and monthly revenue are the same underlying revenue aggregated at different granularities and move together.
The Seasonal Index is computed as current-week sales over average weekly sales, and weekly sales are the aggregation of this store's daily revenue series.
Weekly sales peaks flagged by the seasonal index reliably co-move with spikes in daily order counts on the same store.
Both metrics are the identical computation COUNT(orders) grouped by order_status on Magento, so their per-status counts move together one-to-one.
The fulfillment funnel's stage counts (Invoiced, Shipped, Completed) are derived from the same Magento order statuses, so they co-move with the status distribution buckets.
Refund Count is COUNT(orders WHERE status = refunded), which is exactly the 'refunded' bucket of the status distribution, so the two reliably co-move.
Both rank products by SUM(line_item_revenue) from the same Magento order line items, so the two product-revenue rankings move together.
Each top product's line-item revenue is summed into its product_category, so strong-selling top products drive their category's revenue.
Top products' line-item revenue is a major share of SUM(order_total), so their sales lift total period revenue.
AOV is Total Revenue divided by Total Orders per day, so the daily Magento order count is the denominator component of the AOV formula.
Both measure average order value over the same business, so the Magento AOV trend and the blended AOV move together as the same concept across platforms.
Successful cross-sell recommendations add items to each basket, raising the per-order revenue and thus lifting AOV.
Higher average order value increases the cumulative sum of order totals per customer, pushing customers into higher LTV bands.
The churn risk model's monetary_value input is the per-customer SUM of order totals, which is exactly the LTV computed in LTV Distribution.
The churn risk function takes order_frequency as a direct input feature, the same per-customer order count bucketed by this metric.
Both are ML outputs derived from the same recency/frequency/monetary signals, so high churn-risk tiers reliably co-move with low-value RFM segments.
The churn risk tier feeds the segment_risk_level input of the Marketing Actions priority score, driving which segments get prioritized for intervention.
Customers flagged as high churn risk are precisely those who fail to reorder, so churn-risk levels move inversely with measured cohort retention.
RFM recency/frequency scores reflect repeat-purchase behavior, so high-RFM segments co-move with higher cohort retention.
Higher cohort retention means customers place more repeat orders over time, raising their cumulative lifetime value.
Retained customers keep ordering across months, accumulating the high order totals that populate the top-customers-by-LTV ranking.
Prioritized retention marketing actions target lapsing customers and prompt them to reorder, raising cohort retention.
Successful cross-sell prompts add incremental product purchases per transaction, increasing daily total revenue.
Both compute SUM of order totals per customer from the same order data, differing only in grouping into value ranges vs ranking, so they move together.
Both aggregate the same SUM(order_totals), so revenue grouped by RFM segment co-moves with the LTV distribution.
More orders placed by customers accumulate into higher summed order totals and thus higher LTV.
The monetary value derived from a customer's cumulative order totals is a direct input to RFM segment assignment.
A customer's monetary value (cumulative LTV) is an explicit input feature to the churn-risk scoring function.
Customer LTV feeds the revenue-potential term used to prioritize marketing actions per segment.
The priority score's segment_risk_level input is the customer Risk Tier produced by the Churn Risk Funnel.
The revenue_potential input of the priority score is derived from customer lifetime value as captured by the LTV Distribution.
The customer_count input of the priority score is the distinct customer counts measured by Customer Mix.
The segment_risk_level input requires the RFM segment assignment that RFM Segments produces.
Executing the prioritized marketing actions on targeted segments lifts the revenue generated by those RFM segments.
Both metrics count daily Magento orders with an identical formula, so they move together one-to-one.
The daily order count is the sum of the hourly order counts across the 24 hours of the day.
Total daily orders equal the sum of order counts across all order statuses, which partition the same orders.
Each additional daily order contributes its order total to daily revenue, so more orders drive higher revenue.
Every placed order enters the fulfillment funnel as the top-stage Orders count, feeding all downstream stages.
Higher cart abandonment leaves fewer carts to convert into completed orders, reducing the daily order count.
A larger volume of daily orders produces proportionally more refunded orders downstream.
